note_data.title: Redox reactions
keyPoints: Filtration separates insoluble solid from liquid - liquid particles small enough to go through filter paper pores, solid particles too large
Crystallization is obtaining solid from solid & liquid solution OR formation of crystals from a cooling liquid/saturated solution
Distillation is obtaining pure liquid from solution - pure liquid has low BP and changes to gas easily, solid does not boil so remains in flask
Fractional distillation separates 2 liquids which are mixed (miscible) - liquid with lowest BP comes out first at top of fractionating column
Chromatography separates mixture of organic compounds (eg. colour dyes)
In filtration: solid obtained is called 'Residue', liquid obtained is called 'Filtrate'
In crystallization: process involves heating solution to evaporate solvent, producing hot saturated solution, then crystals of pure solid formed on cooling
In distillation: solution boils, pure liquid turns to vapour, vapour cooled by condenser changes back to liquid called 'Distillate'
The constant temperature of distillation process equals the boiling point of pure liquid

definitions.definition: no more solid can dissolve in it
liquid obtained from distillation, collected in conical flask

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Name the substance that is oxidized/reduced and state the change

modelAnswer.question: Zinc reacts with copper sulfate solution. Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu. Explain what happens to zinc and copper in terms of oxidation and reduction.
modelAnswer.answer: Zinc is oxidized because it loses electrons to form Zn²⁺ ions. Copper is reduced because Cu²⁺ ions gain electrons to form copper atoms. Zinc is the reducing agent because it causes the reduction of copper ions. Copper sulfate is the oxidizing agent because it causes the oxidation of zinc.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Zinc is the reducing agent
Mark 4: Copper sulfate is the oxidizing agent

commonMistakes: Confusing when to use crystallization vs distillation - crystallization obtains solid (solute), distillation obtains liquid (solvent)
Not understanding that fractional distillation separates liquids based on different boiling points
Forgetting that in filtration, the solid particles are too large to pass through filter paper pores
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain (make) crystals, the process will be 'Crystallization'
Remember that the constant temperature of distillation process equals the boiling point of pure liquid
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Displacement reaction setup showing zinc strip in blue copper sulfate solution, with brown copper coating forming on zinc and solution becoming colorless
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (3 marks) — they asked students to calculate oxidation numbers for magnesium and nitrogen in Mg₃N₂, requiring understanding of compound neutrality
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — students had to identify oxidation states of chlorine when Cl₂ reacts with NaOH to form two different Cl-containing products
This concept was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q4 (4 marks) — candidates needed to write equations showing the catalytic role of NO₂ in SO₂ oxidation, demonstrating catalyst regeneration
This was tested in O/N 2023 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — students calculated the change in oxidation state of copper in the reaction 2Cu²⁺(aq) + 4I⁻(aq) → 2CuI(s) + I₂(aq)
This topic appeared in M/J 2022 P2 Q2 (3 marks) — candidates analyzed oxidation number trends and their relationship to melting points using data tables

workedExample.question: The equation for the reaction between aqueous copper ions and aqueous iodide ions is: 2Cu²⁺(aq) + 4I⁻(aq) → 2CuI(s) + I₂(aq). What is the change in oxidation state of copper? (1 mark)
steps: Step 1: Identify the oxidation state of copper in the reactant Cu²⁺(aq). The charge on the ion directly gives us +2
Step 2: Determine the oxidation state of copper in the product CuI(s). Since I has oxidation state -1, and the compound is neutral, Cu must be +1
Step 3: Calculate the change: final oxidation state - initial oxidation state = (+1) - (+2) = -1
Step 4: State the change clearly with correct sign
workedExample.answer: The change in oxidation state of copper is -1 (or decreased by 1)
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct calculation showing copper changes from +2 to +1, giving a change of -1

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused oxidation state with charge notation' — always write oxidation states as +2 or -3, not 2+ or 3- when calculating changes

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot that the sum of oxidation states must equal the overall charge' — always check your oxidation state assignments add up to zero for neutral compounds or to the ion charge
Examiner Report 2024: 'Common error was not showing working for oxidation state calculations' — always show each step when calculating oxidation states, especially for complex ions and compounds
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw electron transfer diagrams showing oxidation (electron loss) and reduction (electron gain) with half-equations. Include: species before and after reaction, electrons shown with e⁻ symbol, arrows indicating electron movement direction, clear labeling of which species is oxidized/reduced, and oxidation states marked above each relevant atom. For example: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation) and Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (reduction).
note_data.quickRecap: Separation methods depend on the physical properties of substances in mixtures. Filtration uses particle size differences, crystallization and distillation use solubility differences, fractional distillation uses boiling point differences, and chromatography separates organic compounds. Each method produces specific products with standard names like residue, filtrate, and distillate.
